data.title: GFLW: Positioned For Slower Growth And Lower Rates
data.description:
The VictoryShares Free Cash Flow Growth ETF (GFLW), launched on 12/03/2024 and managed by Victory Capital Management Inc., provides exposure to large-cap stocks of businesses with growth characteristics, such as a high free cash flow returns on invested capital. It currently manages ~$650 million and charges an expense ratio of 0.39%.

This prioritization of growth in large-cap stocks and the very fact that free cash flow is used to determine it to some extent are what make this fund likely to outperform in the near term. With an economy slowing and a Fed tilting more heavily towards easing as the labor market softens, a growth and FCF bias is warranted. Let's take a look.

First of all, I should note that the idiosyncratic risk is unusually low in this portfolio, especially considering that the total number of holdings is ~100. NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), which is the top holding, accounts for 3.14%, which is not meaningful. Regardless, the top 10 holdings do account for a significant portion of the fund at ~27%, which is typical for a growth ETF.

Not surprisingly, the exposure to tech stocks is much higher than the one you get from broad-market funds; the sector accounts for almost half of the portfolio, which introduces performance drivers that are differentiated from those that are strongly tied to cyclical demand. Another thing to note here is the exposure to Health Care stocks, which are also relatively insulated from cyclical drivers.

This conservative yet aggressive mix may be the reason that helped GFLW perform so well in 2025. That being said, the return differential when you compare it to the S&P 500 or S&P 500 growth isn't meaningful yet.

Going forward, that could change because the lack of meaningful idiosyncratic risk and the high exposure to tech stocks at the same time could provide a balance between a hedge and a bet on non-cyclical drivers in this macro regime, where market uncertainty is still elevated.

We have now entered a regime where economic growth is slower and this may have important implications for the portfolios that are very sensitive to cyclical demand. U.S. real GDP has been volatile this year due to the higher tariffs that were to go into effect, prompting businesses to front-load. The contraction in the first quarter is attributed to an import surge, and the subsequent recovery in the next two quarters is largely the result of a slowdown in imports. In fact, in the first half of 2025, the average GDP growth was 1.4% and consensus expectations suggest that a level close to this will persist in the near term, at least.

This is consistent with the idea that once businesses go through their pre-tariff inventories, the current distortion will cease. The ISM New Orders index also supports this outlook, as it's trending down again and is below 50, indicating slowing demand.

With almost half of GFLW's portfolio being exposed to tech stocks and their less cyclical demand drivers, this is a relative advantage.

It's also interesting to note that monetary policy is now more in favor of growth strategies based on some recent shifts. First of all, it's now clear that the labor market is cooling rapidly, encouraging even lower interest rates to prevent a recession. YoY non-farm payroll growth has decelerated significantly in 2025, now running at only 0.6%. Unemployment, while not historically too high, has also increased meaningfully over the last few months and the rate is now 4.6%. Moreover, weekly initial claims for unemployment insurance have stayed high this year and are now 214K.

The other big shift is much more recent and is inflation stabilizing to some extent. Both CPI and Core CPI growth fell from 3% to around 2.6% on a YoY basis, now meaningfully below the rate consumers anticipate and closer to the target of 2% set by the Fed.

This is likely to keep the Fed on the cutting pace it has maintained in the second half of the year. Before the latest CPI report, inflation was a sticky point when it came to the monetary policy path, as it was (and still is to be fair) that higher tariffs would result in a short-term price increase; this has already taken place to some extent, however, and it's therefore not unlikely that inflation expectations will re-anchor.

Faster easing should be more beneficial for such a growth-biased portfolio and GFLW has the advantage of basing its growth definition on free cash flow, which introduces the quality factor as well. As the market adjusts to a relatively more risk-on environment, this ETF has a good chance to widen its return differential to a degree that makes exposure to it warranted right now.

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

data.title: Optimism Is Muted For Alerus Financial Corporation
data.description:
Among the eight companies in the Diversified Financial Services industry that are scored by Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating, Alerus Financial Corporation (ALRS) is currently ranked at the top. The North Dakota-based firm is also one of the smallest, as one of only two on the list that does not have a market cap of at least $1 billion. ALRS currently has a value of $568.1 million. Only NewtekOne (NEWT) is smaller, with a value of $336 million.

The rating for Alerus, which is right at the bottom end of the Strong Buy range, is based on strong growth numbers for the company in the past year, as well as increasing optimism from analysts for the company’s prospects for the final quarter of 2025. However, long-term shareholders of Alerus may have a hard time believing in its positive review. ALRS has lost over 20% of its value in the last five years. Its stock price is basically trading at the same point as it did in December 2022.

The company made an acquisition of HMN Financial in October of last year. This addition to Alerus's banking operations has provided a boost to the company's earnings in 2025 and also allowed ALRS to make improvements in a number of its profitability metrics. However, some other aspects of Alerus's operations are sliding in the wrong direction. After reviewing the company's results from 2025, I would stay clear of any new investments in ALRS until there is more clarity on the risks that I will discuss in this analysis.

Company Overview

Alerus Financial Corporation operates a network of branches in five states – North Dakota, Minnesota, Wisconsin, Iowa, and Arizona. The company offers traditional banking services but also has business segments for wealth management and retirement services. The company’s CEO, Katie Lorenson, has been in her position since 2022. A map showing the footprint for ALRS, as well as some details for its customer base, is shown below.

Alerus reported $5.33 billion in total assets at the end of Q3 2025. Included in those assets were loans valued at $4.10 billion and an investment portfolio of securities worth almost $792 million. The banking segment of the company controls over 98% of the company’s assets and is responsible for a large majority of its net income as well. A breakdown of the contributions of each business division is shown below.

The largest share of the bank’s loans, 29.3% of the portfolio, was allocated to residential real estate. Non-owner-occupied commercial real estate was second in weight with 21.6%. Commercial and industrial loans were third in portfolio share at 17.1%. A full account of the company’s loans as well as its diversified investment portfolio is illustrated below.

The bank controls about $4.41 billion in customer deposits. That number is 1.7% larger than it was in the previous quarter. Thanks to the HMN Financial acquisition that took place in Q4 2024, deposits have ballooned by 35.3% since the end of Q3 2204. Alerus is paying interest on a large share of those deposits, as only 17.6% are held in non-interest-bearing accounts.

ALRS currently yields 3.76% based on its share price on December 23. Its final dividend for 2025 will be distributed on January 9 to shareholders of record on December 26. The company has 28 consecutive years of dividend growth. Payout increases are traditionally announced in the second quarter of the year. In 2025, the company raised its dividend from $0.20 to $0.21 with the announcement coming in May.

Analysis of Alerus Financial

Alerus Financial posted strong results in the first nine months of 2025. In each of the last three quarters, the company has beaten analysts’ expectations by at least 10%. ALRS earned $16.9 million in Q3, which equates to $0.65 per diluted share. In Q3 2024, the company recorded just $5.2 million in net income. When the results from the first three quarters of this year are added together, Alerus has earned $50.5 million, or $1.95 per share, $1.06 per share more than the same time period last year.

Other profitability metrics for the company are showing improvement as well. Return on average total assets has increased 72 basis points during the first nine months of 2025, while return on tangible common equity has more than doubled. Net interest margin has risen 116 basis points when compared to the same period last year. Alerus has also been able to demonstrate better cost control with an efficiency ratio that is lower by over 12%.

Q1 through Q3 2025

Q1 through Q3 2024

Return on Assets

1.28%

0.56%

Return on TCE

19.25%

8.98%

Net Interest Margin

3.47%

2.31%

Efficiency Ratio

64.81%

77.17%

The addition of the assets from HMN Financial, the holding company for Home Federal Savings Bank, certainly made banking operations more important to Alerus, adding about $1.1 billion in assets to ALRS’s balance sheet including $876.6 in loans. Net interest income in the first nine months of this year totaled $127.3 million. In the same period last year, that number was just $68.8 million. As a result, non-interest income as a percent of revenue fell to 41.1% in Q3 2025 from 54.1% in the same quarter last year.

However, Alerus still gets a significant amount of revenue from non-banking operations. Total non-interest income totaled $29.4 million in Q3, with retirement services collecting 56% of that total. Wealth advisory and banking operations split the remainder almost evenly.

After taking a hit with the HMN acquisition at the end of last year, the company has been able to recover its losses in tangible book value. TBV for ALRS stood at $16.50 at the end of Q3 2024. It was just $16.11 after Q2 this year. It climbed up almost 5% in Q3 to reach $16.90. Its stock now trades at 1.31x TBV.

Risks to Consider

Asset quality has become an increasing concern for Alerus Financial over the last four quarters. Its ratio for non-performing assets stands at 1.13%, seven basis points lower than it was at the end of last year. The ratio of reserves to total loans was 1.51% at the end of Q3, one basis point higher than Q4 2024. Its reserves as a share of non-performing loans have fallen to 104%, a small improvement from the end of last year but a decline of over 300% from the end of 2023.

Total nonperforming assets were $48.0 million as of September 30, 2024, increasing by over 450% from the previous quarter. A large piece of that increase, $25 million, was caused by one construction loan moving to non-accrual in the second quarter of that year. An additional $13.6 million was caused by a similar action with a large residential real estate relationship and one non-owner-occupied CRE loan. During its Q3 2025 conference call, the company announced that it was making a continued effort to "derisk the balance sheet," and referred to the recent sale of some of its higher-risk hospitality loans.

In addition to asset quality, another concern for Alerus Financial is its overall outlook for FY 2026. Earnings are expected to decline next year. The company is predicted to report a profit of $2.53 per share this year. However, the five analysts that cover ALRS have an average estimate of just $2.44 per share for earnings next year. Even the highest estimate for FY 2026 is just $2.50, $0.03 below the total for this year. That makes the PE multiple for the company, which currently stands at 8.74, look somewhat less attractive.

As part of its Q3 2025 financial report, Alerus gave its own preliminary guidance for next year. Loan growth is expected in the mid-single digits. Net interest margin is expected to improve, but only by a few basis points. The company said its return on assets would actually decline by about five basis points.

One final risk for ALRS is something that must be considered for most all stocks in its sector. Banks and other financial services companies are sensitive to shifts in economic activity that could be related to interest rates, rising inflation, and higher unemployment. These factors could lead the US into a recession in 2026, which would impact Alerus's banking operations and could also cause outflows in the assets under management from Alerus’s wealth management division. Even though the company is diversified into multiple states, it is more subject to vulnerability in the states where it conducts the bulk of its business – Minnesota and North Dakota.

Below is a chart that shows the location assigned to Alerus's non-owner-occupied CRE and multi-family CRE loans. Minnesota represents over half of those loans, and its economic outlook is less than robust. Slow growth in the state's labor force will inhibit job creation and reduce the capacity for GDP growth.

The Bottom Line

Based on the balance between perceived risks and rewards for Alerus Financial, I rate its stock as a Hold heading into the new year. The growth numbers that have elevated Seeking Alpha's Quant analysis will have much tougher comparisons next year and likely bring down that assessment in closer alignment with my own. I do not see compelling catalysts to move ALRS ahead of its peers, with some of its financial metrics getting worse in the next 12 months. Therefore, I am recommending avoiding any new investments in the stock at this time.

data.title: Barings BDC: It Is Time To Enter This 11.7% Yielder
data.description:
Since February, 2025, I have been bearish on Barings BDC Inc (BBDC), arguing that for the given discount and earnings power, there are better alternatives in the business development company aka BDC space - i.e., not worth wasting capital here in BBDC.

Since then, BBDC's share price has decreased by ~13%. Granted, on a total return basis, the performance looks better - negative 2%. And in the context of the overall BDC sector (BIZD), what BBDC has delivered could actually be considered a decent result.

Plus, given that during the year BBDC has experienced this kind of share price decline, it means that on a P/NAV basis the entry point has become more compelling than before. Sure, there has been some NAV contraction in the process, but it does not stand even close to the changes in the share price.

Currently, BBDC trades at a P/NAV of 0.81x, which is exactly in line with the BDC sector median multiple (also 0.81x). The dividend yield stands at 11.7%, while the base dividend coverage statistic 123% (i.e., one of the strongest levels in the game).

So, the question is: could it be so that BBDC finally presents a compelling investment case that could even outcompete the offering that is put on the table from such first-lien focused quality gems as Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC) and Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL)?

Let's unpack the case now.

Thesis review

I will start with the negatives, which to a large extent has so far motivated me to skip making an investment in BBDC.

For already 5 consecutive quarters, BBDC is dealing with unfavorable mark downs. The net unrealized and realized loss component has put a meaningful downward pressure on the NAV. For example, in Q3, 2025, the NAV per share declined by $0.08 exactly because of additional write downs, which inherently stem from non-performing (or struggling) loan investment businesses.

However, if we look at the non-accrual statistics, then we will not actually notice anything bad. In all of the previous 5 quarters, the non-accrual share of the total portfolio fair value has remained well-below 1%, which is much better than for the sector average (of around 2.5%). The thing is that the mark downs, which BBDC has recognized have not (yet) been so bad where the underlying companies would be forced to completely halt their debt service.

Yet, it is BDC investing 101 to assume these mark downs as a sign or indicative of potential non-accrual problems going forward.

Another aspect, which makes me nervous is the elevated debt profile. BBDC ended Q3, 2025 with a net debt to equity of 1.26x, which implies that there is almost no spare debt capacity available to expand the portfolio. Plus, if some non-accruals start to indeed emerge, having relatively aggressive leverage will simply exacerbate the consequences.

Finally, we have to remember that BBDC is a pure-play lender. In other words, it has not introduced any bias towards equity co-investments as Fidus Investment (FDUS), Main Street Capital (MAIN) or even Ares Capital (ARCC), which is commonly deemed as first and second-lien focused BDC. And what this means is that the base rates cuts that were realized in October, November and December will significantly compress the current NII per share generation without providing any benefit to BBDC's portfolio. For example, in FDUS's case we could at least make an argument that while its loan investments will produce lower income streams, the equity investment monetizations could come in as notable offsets (i.e., the lower the base rates, the lower the discount rates, the higher the valuations).

Having said that, there are a couple of items, which really look interesting.

First, as mentioned above, BBDC's dividend coverage is 123%, which enables a non-dilutive equity financed growth - critical for BBDC given its exhausted balance sheet. Plus, such kind of base dividend coverage per definition serves as a strong cushion for absorbing forthcoming pressures from lower base rates.

So, I would say that if there are no negative surprises on the non-accrual front, the current dividend of 11.7% should be safe at least throughout the 2026 (even with lower base rate backdrop in mind).

And speaking of the lower base rates and non-accruals, let's remember that there is a relevant relationship between these two. Namely, when the rates are lower, it becomes easier for BDC loan investment companies to service debt, which is positive for non-accrual risk mitigation. As of Q3, 2025, the weighted average interest coverage for BBDC portfolio companies stood at 2.4x, which could be considered a solid level.

Now, and this is, arguably, the most important item to appreciate - BBDC has very favorable fee profile. For instance, the incentive fee hurdle rate for BBDC is 8.25%, which is clearly above sector average. And it is exactly the incentive fee component, which accounts for the lion's share of fees paid (in good years).

In Q3, we could already see how this has worked in practice.

While the total income slightly declined from the Q2 level, the NII per share increased from $0.28 (in Q2) to $0.32 in Q3. In the meantime, most BDCs registered declining NII per share results in their Q3 reports. The main reason why the NII per share experienced this kind of move was the ~50% reduction in the quarterly incentive fee stemming from the unrealized losses (which is what many BDCs do not include in their IRR hurdle rate metrics at all).

The bottom line

In my view, BBDC's investment offering has improved relative to where it was back in February, 2025 when I established a rather bearish view on this BDC.

BBDC trades now at a significant discount, while providing one of the strongest dividend coverage levels in the sector. We can also see how its shareholder-friendly fee profile has kicked in, helping the NII per share generation remain stable amid struggling NAV and earnings.

The key risk that could blow up this case is the emergence of notable non-accruals. Such scenario is not that unlikely given the systematic net realized and unrealized losses. However, I see three offset for this:

  1. Significant NII surplus over the dividend distributions, acting as a cushion for shock absorption.
  2. Lower base rates helping the underlying portfolio companies manage their cash flows and keep BBDC's loan service intact.
  3. Favorable fee profile, which in the case of further NAV losses would provide an additional support for the NII (via lowered incentive fee).

Personally, I am not going long BBDC now because I have already a considerable concentration in KBDC, FDUS and other high-quality BDCs.

However, if I was looking for a new BDC addition to my portfolio, I would most likely dedicate a small share to BBDC for sure.

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: The Cash Flow Age Is Finally Here
data.description:
Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD) is trying a new playbook. The company has historically driven reliable growth through lofty spending on high-return growth projects. Management appears to be signaling a shift in capital allocation policy. Management has guided for a sharp reduction in capital spending just as the growth projects of the last several years are set to come online. The net result may be a new cash flow age, with these earmarked for faster distribution growth and even unit buybacks. I reiterate my Buy rating for the stock.

EPD Stock Price

I last covered EPD in December 2024, where I rated the stock a Buy as a “surprising beneficiary of the new political regime.” The stock is up since, but has underperformed the broader market.

The stock may be due for a re-rating as management looks poised to increase unitholder returns.

EPD Stock Key Metrics

EPD is one of the largest midstream companies. It is diversified but primarily known for transporting natural gas liquids ("NGLs") which make up over 50% of gross operating margin.

The energy sector is known to be highly price sensitive with a great deal of cyclicality, but EPD has been able to deliver consistent cash flow growth even through periods of oil price weakness. This is due to the company primarily earning fees on the volumes transported along its pipelines.

That consistent cash flow growth has led to 27 consecutive years of distribution growth. The company had intentionally slowed distribution growth in recent years in order to reduce leverage and increase its distribution coverage. Distribution coverage stood at 1.7x as of late, far higher than the 1.2x range in the years prior to 2017.

The company has undergone an aggressive slate of growth projects as it has utilized the vast majority of retained cash flow for growth CapEx.

That may soon change. Management is forecasting growth CapEx to decline by roughly 50% to around $2.5 billion in 2026. This comes as many of the projects that the company has been working on are expected to come online in the next few years.

This isn’t the first time that management has promised a decline in growth CapEx. Looking back at my past reports, management had given guidance for $2.5 billion of CapEx for the 2024 year, only to later end up far exceeding that number. This time feels different, though. Management’s presentations appear to show a greater highlight for buybacks and distribution growth. I note that the company has also increased the size of its buyback program to $5 billion.

I view this development with mixed feelings. On the one hand, a ramp up in buyback activity would be the most obvious catalyst for a re-rating. On the other hand, this is a management team that has generated stellar returns on their growth investments. I am hopeful that any pause in growth projects might end if the equity cost of capital eventually declines.

In the most recent quarter, the company did see not-so-modest declines in cash flows.

On the conference call, management noted that the results were largely due to delays that have since been addressed. Management noted “a lot of upside that was pushed out of the third quarter.” Management noted that they “expect an inflection point in discretionary free cash flow in 2026 as we have completed a 4-year period of large investments.” Again - their commentary does appear to indicate that we should believe them this time. Management noted that distribution growth will be their “primary focus” but they expanded the buyback program to enhance their “flexibility to grow buybacks alongside rising free cash flow.” Management noted that their leverage ratio of 3.3x debt to EBITDA, while still conservative, is above their target range of 2.75x to 3.25x but is expected to return into that range by the end of 2026. Management expects growth CapEx to stay in the “$2 billion, $2.5 billion range” over the next “2 or 3 years.” Management stated that it “could” get to $2.7 billion but that they “don’t see it going to $3 billion.” The buyback age may be upon us.

Is EPD Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, EPD traded at around the low end of its yield range over the past 5 years. That said, the yield is still higher than the roughly 6% yield that it traded at pre-pandemic.

With management now guiding for growth CapEx to come in at around $2 billion to $2.5 billion annually moving forward, the stock’s 12x P/E ratio is now more applicable given that depreciation and amortization comes in at around that range. Consensus estimates call for the company to deliver solid earnings growth over the coming years.

Given management’s track record of delivering strong ROIC, I expect the roughly 1.5% in retained earnings yield to lead to at least 1.5% in earnings growth. In conjunction with modest inflation, this should allow for roughly 2% to 3% earnings growth on the low end on a consistent basis. I note that the company is likely to see outsized earnings growth in the near term due to growth projections coming online. Assuming distribution growth settles in the 2% to 4% range, I expect the stock to re-rate to a 6% yield or lower. That valuation would still look conservative given the resilient business model and conservative balance sheet.

My outlook for the next several years is that as management prioritizes growing the distribution and unit buybacks over growth CapEx, the stock might re-rate to as low as a 5% to 5.5% yield, at which point management might feel more comfortable reigniting their growth CapEx spending partially funded by some equity sales. The stock is offering a generous yield as one waits for the acceleration in growth.

EPD Stock Risks

It is possible that management changes their mind and ramps up growth CapEx. I note that this would not be entirely a bad thing, but the market is unlikely to be enthusiastic given that I expect many are now expecting otherwise. It is possible that the energy markets enter a cyclical downturn. While EPD does not have full direct exposure to declines in price, volumes can experience pressure in such a downturn. It is possible that the stock sees its discount relative to c-corps widen if the market grows wary of potential unitholder risks. I of course must note that the company issues a k-1 tax form which investors must take into account for their personal tax situations.

EPD Stock Conclusion

Investors may be wary of management’s guide for reduced capital spending. I think this time is different. EPD may be set to accelerate distribution growth and unit buybacks. The investor materials appear to indicate a stronger commitment to this outlook than in the past. I expect this revamped capital allocation policy to lead to a tangible re-rating in the stock. EPD is a core holding in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my Buy rating for the stock.

data.title: IDVO: I Say 'Oui' To This International Covered Call Fund
data.description:
I may have been a little harsh back in February when I said that DIVO was having a "hard time standing out in today's market." It wasn't anything against Amplify ETFs in particular. It's just that, with all the US equity-based covered call funds on the market, I didn't see a clear reason why DIVO would be taken over others. And I mostly maintain that statement today, though I think I may have given it a buy in retrospect. The hold was a little harsh, but the other points I made still stand.

Either way, my point in bringing that up is to introduce an Amplify ETF that I think doesn't have a hard time standing out at all, and in fact stood out to me very quickly: the Amplify CWP International Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (IDVO).

The reason I say it doesn't have a hard time standing out is because there are very few covered call funds that focus only on international stocks. When building out a portfolio of equity income products, it feels natural to look for geographic diversity, just as you would in any given stock portfolio if you felt like you were overconcentrated in the U.S.

But there are very few options. In fact, the only other option I can find is a NEOS ETFs fund, NIHI, that was launched very recently and so lacks a lot of history.

But IDVO has been out for a couple of years now, so I believe it warrants taking a look at it, especially if international continues to perform next year as it has this year. It's had a killer year, especially compared to its U.S. counterpart, DIVO.

IDVO Overview

Inception 9/8/2022
Price $38.80
Distribution Rate 5.85%
Dividend Amount (TTM) $2.04
Dividend Frequency Monthly
19a-1 RoC % ~75%
Assets Under Management $604M
Expense Ratio 0.66%

IDVO has a fairly straightforward strategy: buy international stocks and sell call options against those stocks. This creates a higher immediate income stream while also providing exposure to ex-U.S. equities. The folks at Amplify ETFs do their options writing opportunistically. So instead of writing against an index, they write against individual companies in the portfolio. This allows them more flexibility, but also typically provides less income and more appreciation.

We can see this trade-off in the returns breakdown, where the total return, which accounts for dividends paid and reinvested, is far above the price return.

Compare it to an equities-only ex-US fund, and you can see a clear difference in how returns are factored with the income component. Here's its lifespan compared to VXUS, a fairly standard ETF for those seeking international exposure.

IDVO Holdings

The fund's major holdings are spread across the globe and include all markets except for the U.S., although it does hold firms that do business in the U.S., but are headquartered elsewhere.

Here are the top holdings. Note that the fund does not own the ex-US at market weights, but instead has its own active take on foreign markets. Also notable, there is a lot of Chinese exposure, starting with the largest holding, Alibaba (BABA).

Amplify ETFs use a company called Capital Wealth Planning ("CWP") to be its sub-advisor. They are the ones who actively manage not only the securities in the portfolio but also the covered call writing. Here is their methodology:

Here's the country breakdown: China leads as the largest exposure, but beyond that, there are sizable allocations to Canadian, European like the UK, Germany, and Spain; and other East Asian markets, like Taiwan and Japan.

One of the things many people find preferable about ex-US stocks is that they tend to have a lower weight in technology stocks. While technology is still a major player in international markets, it is not as heavily weighted in IDVO as it is in US-based funds. The largest sector here is finance.

The fund is still a large cap ETF and holds more than 87% of its assets in large cap stocks, although notably there is an allocation to some mid-caps, with virtually no small- or micro-cap exposure.

As expected by its performance, the fund has only seen weekly inflows this year. Only missing one week of positive inflows out of the entire year, and that one week was a net even flow, not an outflow. That shows a lot of investor confidence, but also is a sign of investor money chasing past performance to some degree.

IDVO Dividends

International stocks are typically more likely to pay dividends than U.S.-based stocks. This is notable for IDVO since it has a large chunk of its dividend coming from the underlying companies in the portfolio as well as its own covered call strategy.

Of the total 5.85% distribution rate, about 1.73% of it is made up of ordinary income.

Options income when paid through the ETF vehicle is often considered return of capital ("RoC"), which is just an accounting feature granted by the IRS in this case, and is not necessarily destructive like it may be when distributed by closed-end funds. When distributions come out, the return of capital portion is deducted from one's cost basis, and so is not taxable in the year it is received, necessarily. Instead, the taxes are deferred to whenever the shares are sold, and shares held longer than one year would qualify for long-term capital gains taxes. This rate is usually lower than one's ordinary income rate, and so it's preferable to earn RoC from ETFs. Should be of note here that if your cost basis hits zero, then any further distributions would be taxable in the year they are received but automatically qualify for long-term capital gains tax rates.

Here is IDVO's latest 19a-1 form, an estimate of the breakdown between the two kinds of income received. They're estimating that November's distribution is about 70% RoC, and that for the year they've managed 76%.

Conclusion

International stocks look poised to continue their run into next year, and so when searching for income-related funds, very few are available to investors and even fewer have long track records (the other competitor I can find came out three months ago!)

IDVO stands out here, with a 3-year record, offering a helpful tool for those who want ex-US exposure to diversify their stock portfolios, but don't want to give up higher income. While IDVO's options are a little conservative, leading to a distribution rate that is lower than many other covered call funds, it's greater upside participation has served it well so far, and this income fund has beaten the international index which is a feat on its own.

I'm giving IDVO a buy rating, and considering revisiting my hold rating on DIVO, as I've come around to the Amplify ETFs method of selling tactical covered calls. In retrospect, I was a little harsh the first time, so I'm glad I've come into this review of IDVO with that lens. For investors that want to diversify their exposure and keep a higher income, this is absolutely a fund worth considering.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: The Sprott Physical Gold Trust In 2025 And The Outlook For 2026
data.description:
As of December 26, 2025, gold (XAUUSD:CUR) was the worst-performing precious metal of the four trading on the CME’s COMEX and NYMEX division. Silver (XAGUSD:CUR) at $76.50 per ounce on the nearby futures contract was 161.6% higher since the end of 2024. Platinum (XPTUSD:CUR) futures at $2,473 per ounce were 171.6% higher since the end of last year, while nearby palladium (XPDUSD:CUR) futures at $2,014 were 121.4% higher over the period. While gold’s performance lagged behind the other three precious metals, as it was the only one that did not more than double, it was still impressive.

The Sprott Physical Gold Trust (PHYS) owns physical gold bullion, located in the hub of international gold trading.

Gold's Price Soars In 2025

Nearby COMEX gold futures settled 2024 at $2,641 per ounce, after reaching a record high of $2,801.80 in October 2024.

The monthly continuous COMEX gold futures chart shows that it did not take long for the precious metal to rise to a new record high, eclipsing the October 2024 peak in January 2025. The bullish price action continued throughout 2025. Meanwhile, as of Friday, December 26, gold’s price of $4,569.70 per ounce was more than 73% higher than the end of December 2024 closing level. While silver, platinum, and palladium have outperformed gold in 2025, the yellow metal has posted an explosive gain.

Uncharted Territory

Gold’s rally began at the end of the last century, when the price fell to a 1999 low of $252.80 per ounce. The U.K. government auctioned half the country’s gold reserves, causing gold prices to fall. Other governments did not follow the U.S., and gold spent the next twenty-six years making higher lows and higher highs.

The quarterly continuous contract COMEX gold futures chart shows that gold was not the barbarous relic the U.K. assumed, as the price has risen by more than 18 times from its 1999 low. Meanwhile, central banks and governments have been buying gold and adding to reserves over the past years, fueling the bullish price trend. Governments continue to validate gold’s role as a reserve asset, classifying it as a foreign currency reserve. Ironically, and perhaps insultingly, the hub of the international gold market is London in the United Kingdom, the country that sold half its golden treasure at prices below $300 per ounce.

Meanwhile, gold’s price eclipsed the 1980 high of $875 in 2008, and the bullish market continues to reach new all-time highs in late 2025, with the price over 5 times higher than the 1980 peak. Gold at over $4,500 is in uncharted territory, as the bullish freight train shows no sign of a correction.

PHYS Holds Allocated Good Delivery Gold Bars In London

The fund summary for the Sprott Physical Gold Trust states:

PHYS’s top holdings include:

As the chart shows, PHYS holds virtually all of its assets in fully allocated, physical 400-ounce gold bars that are acceptable for delivery in the London market. A physically allocated gold bar comes from an approved refinery, and each bar has an individual serial number. 0.9999% pure gold is the highest purity.

At $34.57 per share, PHYS had $15.85 billion in assets under management. PHYS trades an average of more than 4.1 million shares daily and charges a 0.41% management fee.

PHYS Has Kept Pace With Gold Futures In 2025

On December 26, 2025, the high of $4,584 per ounce was 73.57% above the end-of-2024 closing price.

The chart shows that the PHYS ETF moved 72.94% higher over the period, from $20.14 at the end of last year to $34.83 per share on December 26, 2025. The slightly lower increase was due to the management fee, which covers storage, insurance, and other expenses related to holding physical, allocated gold bullion.

The Bullish And Bearish Cases For Gold In 2026

The bullish case for gold and the PHYS ETF in 2026 includes:

  • Gold’s price trend remains highly bullish at the end of 2025, and the trend is always a trader’s or investor’s best friend.
  • The U.S. dollar and all fiat currencies’ purchasing power is deteriorating, supporting even higher gold prices in 2026.
  • The U.S. debt, at over $38.5 trillion, remains a bullish factor.
  • Inflation remains above the U.S. Federal Reserves’ 2% target, which remains bullish.
  • Central bank gold buying continues, despite the price level.
  • Gold has become the second-largest reserve asset, surpassing the euro and ranking second only to the U.S. dollar.
  • Sanctions and tariffs are trade barriers that encourage central banks worldwide to increase their gold reserves.

The bearish case for gold and the PHYS ETF in 2026 includes:

  • Commodity cyclicality suggests that prices rise to levels at which production increases, inventories rise, and demand declines, eventually reaching tops.
  • Even the most aggressive bull markets rarely move in straight lines, and corrections are likely.
  • The higher gold’s price rises, the greater the odds of a correction.

Gold is a unique asset, part commodity and part financial asset. Therefore, the bullish case for gold remains compelling as the market enters 2026, the twenty-seventh year of its bull market.

PHYS holds physical, allocated gold bullion, making it a pure play on gold’s performance. I rate PHYS a Strong Buy for 2026. However, buying gold on price corrections has been optimal since 1999, and I expect that trend to continue.

data.title: Ondas: Winning The Drone Wars In 2026
data.description:
Thesis Summary

The U.S. drone market is approaching a very important key inflection point, which could really supercharge companies in the sector in 2026.

Regulation is tightening, defense budgets are expanding, and demand is shifting.

2026 is shaping up as the year when early prototypes give way to scaled deployment.

Ondas Holdings Inc. (ONDS) sits squarely in the middle of that transition.

The company has rapidly repositioned itself from a collection of promising technologies into a well-capitalized company operating across multiple areas of interest.

Revenues are beginning to take off, and investors are beginning to notice.

While ONDS is certainly a high-risk speculative investment, I believe drone technology might be one of the best investing themes in the next year.

The 2026 Drone Inflection

A few weeks ago, the Federal Communications Commission added foreign-made drones and critical drone components to its Covered List following a national security determination.

The practical implication is clear. Future drones operating legally in the United States will increasingly be built with domestic components. This is a security and strategic priority.

At the same time, funding is starting to spring up.

The 2026 defense budget also points to billions of dollars of incremental unmanned systems spending. But this doesn’t mean consumer-style drones that we picture today.

This funding is skewed toward military applications, counter-UAS systems, and infrastructure protection.

The drone sector has the implied backing of the Federal government, and it will only grow in strategic importance over the next few years.

Ondas’ Strategic Pivot: OAS as the Growth Engine

This is where Onds comes in. The stock is up over 300% in the last year, and I believe there's a good reason for it.

Ondas has been executing, while also undergoing a strategic shift that will better position it going forward.

ONDS has consolidated its defense and security ambitions under Ondas Autonomous Systems (OAS), shifting from single-product offerings toward a “system-of-systems” model spanning air, ground, sensing, and communications.

The Q3 results show momentum and strong guidance.

In Q3, revenues came in at just over $10 million, up more than 500% YoY.

Ondas also secured a backlog of over $23 million, which is very encouraging.

On top of that, full-year 2025 revenue guidance was recently raised to $36 million in the last quarter, and the 2026 revenue target is at least $110 million.

Breaking it down by segment, Counter-UAS is emerging as the lead vertical.

However, this is all tied in Ondas's broader technological platform, what I earlier referred to as the system of systems approach.

Rather than selling isolated hardware, Ondas is building an installed base of interoperable systems that can be expanded over time with software, services, upgrades, and additional capability layers.

This approach mirrors how defense and critical-infrastructure customers actually procure: long-lived platforms, not one-off devices.

The key message here is that is this is not just a compilation of technologies and corporate entities. We are building a scalable unified service delivery platform designed to service demanding customers and use cases and importantly, accelerate revenue growth and our path to profitability.

Source: Earnings Call.

Balance Sheet Is Strong

This is still a young market, with losses expected. But Ondas has now secured a strong financial position.

The company raised approximately $855 million in 2025, leaving it exceptionally well capitalized relative to peers, which gives Ondas some great strengths.

Strong liquidity allows Ondas to bridge delays without starving operations. Moreover, Defense markets reward reliability and longevity.

Future Outlook

ONDS has laid out a clear target of $110 million in revenues next year.

Management also believes gross margins should eventually trend towards the 50% due to improvements in scale and a better revenue mix. However, profitability is still a long-term objective.

For now, the company has to focus on winning new contracts, expanding its backlog and delivering competitive products and systems.

There's not a lot of analyst coverage of ONDS, but the consensus seems to point towards very strong revenue growth over the next two years.

The global drone market size is expected to reach $163.60 billion by 2030, which is at a CAGR of 14.3% over the next five years.

Not crazy growth overall, but as I said, this is accelerating in the U.S., and it still leaves a lot of room for a company like Ondas to generate billions of dollars a year eventually.

Risks

Despite the tailwinds, there are meaningful risks.

Integrating multiple acquisitions into a coherent platform is complex, so there is definitely execution risk.

Also, investors should consider that Defense budgets are large but slow, and timing mismatches can frustrate markets.

Margins could also be volatile, and profitability is not a priority right now, leading to further dilution risk.

Lastly, larger defense contractors and well-funded peers will surely try to catch up.

Final Thoughts

All in all, ONDS is a speculative but worthwhile buy in my opinion.

This is a company that is quickly growing revenues in an interesting market that should only pick up steam in the coming years.

The valuation, at over 13x 2027 revenues, is not cheap, but a growth story like this is worth holding onto for a few years.

If things play out as management expects, ONDS will easily become a multibillion-dollar revenue company.

data.title: Oral Wegovy Approval Is The Critical 2026 Lifeline Novo Nordisk Needs
data.description:
Intro

When I wrote the analysis “The GLP-1 Oral Frontier: Novo Nordisk And Eli Lilly's 100-Year Duel Enters Its Next Chapter,” back in September, it focused on two long‑time rivals entering a new phase of their century‑long competition by expanding the weight‑loss market from injectable drugs to oral pills. The potential here is substantial: easier use, better accessibility, and a much larger addressable market as people who are uncomfortable with injections come into play as well.

With the FDA approval of oral Wegovy announced on December 23, it’s fair to say that Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) and Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) have officially received the starting signal for this new chapter of the race.

Although the approval was widely expected and presumably priced in, Novo’s stock still rallied nearly 10% after the announcement, avoiding the often occurring sell-the-news phenomenon in such cases. That could be taken as a sign that the market really appreciates clarity in that situation, and since the decision came so close to year‑end, some uncertainty may have emerged about whether the approval would come before the New Year, or if unexpected issues had arisen that could delay FDA clearance.

In the end, the market still received its expected outcome and gave shareholders a much-appreciated Christmas gift as the emerging doubts were cleared away.

Recent Dynamics

Since my initial analysis of the oral race, both Novo and Eli Lilly have released several updates on their oral programs in the meantime, each highlighting the superiority of their own oral candidates. For an investor who doesn’t keep up with every detail in that space closely, the current state of things may feel a bit confusing. The latest headlines highlight the superiority of Novo’s pill over Lilly’s rival orforglipron, though not long ago some reports claimed the opposite.

Before diving into the figures and dynamics, it’s important to clarify something that often confuses investors: there are two distinct treatment areas — obesity in patients with diabetes, often additionally labeled as Type 2 diabetes, and obesity in patients without diagnosed diabetes. When discussing GLP‑1 drugs in the diabetes context, we are essentially talking about the treatment of Type 2 diabetes, which accounts for roughly 95% of all diabetes cases. Type 1 diabetes, by contrast, is an autoimmune condition that typically begins earlier in life and follows its own separate treatment pathway.

The newly approved oral Wegovy falls under the weight-loss or obesity-management category. That market category relies more on out-of-pocket spending compared to the diabetes segment, as obesity by itself is less often diagnosed as a medical condition, making reimbursement harder to obtain. Diabetes treatments often come with higher initial price tags, which are typically offset by private insurance or government subsidies.

Still, the weight‑loss or obesity without diabetes segment has the potential for explosive volume growth. Even if margins are lower, with fewer reimbursement possibilities, the scale of the market opportunity is enormous, given that a very significant share of the global population is at least somewhat overweight or at risk of becoming so.

WHO reports:

  • Worldwide adult obesity has more than doubled since 1990, and adolescent obesity has quadrupled.
  • In 2022, 2.5 billion adults (43% of the world’s population aged 18 and older) were overweight. Of these, 890 million (16%) were living with obesity.

An additional illustration from the company’s presentation is a great example of obesity management market opportunities compared to other segments in the U.S.:

ASCVD - Atherosclerotic Cardiovascular Disease.

CKD - Chronic Kidney Disease.

Furthermore, GLP-1 treatments have gained real-world recognition for potentially helping to reduce various addictions, including alcohol use.

Recently approved oral Wegovy has the same active ingredient as Rybelsus, with the difference coming from the dosage

I believe one more clarification would be helpful to add here - the same molecule or ingredient, as it's also often used for explanation, in the newly approved oral Wegovy, semaglutide, is already approved for diabetes in an oral form called Rybelsus, though at a lower dose. Besides having different brand names, oral Wegovy contains 25 mg of semaglutide, while Rybelsus contains only 14 mg.

Lilly leads the oral diabetes segment

While Rybelsus has secured its place in the diabetes market being the only oral GLP-1 drug ever approved and offers several compelling characteristics, its impact on weight loss is relatively modest at around 5%, placebo-adjusted. That’s the weakness of Rybelsus Lilly used to declare victory on September 17 — the headline that sent the Lilly stock soaring on that day: "Lilly’s oral GLP‑1, orforglipron, superior to oral semaglutide in head‑to‑head trial." While the headline is technically correct, it fails to make clear that the comparison was with the older, already approved Rybelsus, not the higher‑dose oral Wegovy that was still seeking approval at the time.

Study results as reported by Eli Lilly:

Looking at the oral Wegovy, or semaglutide 25mg, diabetes trial results from the PIONEER study, we see a placebo-adjusted weight loss of 7.6%. That’s a solid improvement, but it still falls short compared to the results seen with orforglipron. That said, while the orforglipron headline result is labeled as the "efficacy estimand," the press release also includes the "treatment-regimen estimand" result, which is notably lower, at 8.2% weight loss for 36 mg of orforglipron, which is nearly comparable to 7.6% for 25 mg of semaglutide.

To sum it up, I think it’s fair to say that while the long‑time rivals are eager to claim victories with their upcoming oral pills meant for diabetes, both orforglipron and the higher‑dose semaglutide ultimately deliver rather similar and modest weight-loss results, only a few percentage points better than Rybelsus, the only currently marketed oral diabetes pill, and both falling behind effectivness shown by similar injectables.

Novo is leading the weight loss management market for patients without diabetes

The newly approved oral Wegovy — or semaglutide 25 mg, if you prefer — holds the advantage of being the first to market, but that lead is expected to be short‑lived, likely only around three months, as Lilly was awarded the special “national priority” voucher by the FDA.

The headlines tout a 16.6% weight-loss result for the Wegovy pill, but it’s worth taking a closer look at the details to see how it truly lives up against orforglipron in all aspects considered. I marked the superior comparisons in bold.

Comparison of oral weight‑loss pills Novo Lilly
Wegovy 25mg Orforglipron 36mg
Headline weight loss achieved 16.6% 12.4%
Weight loss placebo adj. 13.9% 11.5%
body weight reductions ≥10% 59.6%
body weight reductions ≥15% 56.1% 39.6%
≥20% 34.4%
Total side effects and placebo-adjusted
Nausea % 46.6/28 33.7/23.3
Constipation % 20.1/10.3 25.4/16.1
Diarrhea % 17.6/8.8 23.1/13.5
Vomiting % 30.9/25 24/20.5
Discontinuation AE % 6.9/1 10.3/7.7

Source: Author's Creation.

To summarize here, while it might seem that the Wegovy pill didn’t fully live up to its big headline win, once the results are adjusted for placebo and the side‑effect profiles taken into account, with some points that favor Wegovy and others that favor Lilly’s pill. Despite this, Wegovy still seems to have a much stronger overall profile, with 90.5% of trial participants achieving at least 15% weight loss, compared to Lilly's 39.6%, which is a significantly better result.

Summary of the current state of the oral race

It’s a bit strange why there are such big differences in weight loss data between the diabetes treatment segment and just obesity. The fact is, while Novo has superior metrics with its very recent approval, it lags in the diabetes segment with the same drug. On top of that, Lilly has a clear advantage with its pill, orforgliporn, which has no restrictions on when and how it can be administered, unlike Novo’s pill that must be taken in the morning on an empty stomach, followed by a 30-minute wait before eating or drinking.

All in all, I think we’ll have to let real‑world experience determine who ultimately wins the pill race. Just to add some color to the awards here - if current research is accurate, the total oral pills addressable market is nearly $30 billion by 2030, assuming these estimates hold up.

To provide even more context here - Rybelsus has generated about $2.7 billion in sales for Novo so far this year, which would annualize to roughly $3.6 billion, leaving plenty of room for further growth.

Dynamics from the Q3/Year to Date results

While Q3 results came in ahead of expectations, the company narrowed its full‑year outlook range downward, leading to a mixed market reaction with the stock trading sideways.

Looking at Novo’s broader portfolio, approximately 68% of revenue comes from diabetes treatments and 26% from obesity management. At the same time, the obesity segment is expanding much faster — growing 41% year over year compared with 10% growth in GLP‑1 diabetes treatments, while overall sales increased by 16% to 229.920 billion DKK/~$33.32 billion in the first nine months of 2025.

The GLP-1 market size and growth YoY:

..and the obesity segment's size and growth YoY:

The broader developments roadmap as presented by Novo:

Risks and Opportunities Heading Into 2026

Risks

The competitive landscape is becoming more challenging, as Lilly’s portfolio continues to grow significantly faster.

The company itself presents the situation on their latest earnings presentation where we see that Novo's 19% growth lags the market overall 39% growth :

The market’s growth is driven by the combined results of Novo and Lilly, as there’s currently no other competition in the GLP-1 segment.

Margin pressures are likely to grow as Novo’s semaglutide loses patent protection in countries like Canada, India, China, Brazil, and Turkey. This may trigger new creative cross-border activities that could add even more pressure on prices.

Opportunities

With the recent approval of oral Wegovy, Novo gains a meaningful early‑mover advantage in the oral obesity‑management space, which could allow it to build a strong position before orforglipron enters the market.

While this is admittedly a bold assumption, there is a real possibility that the market is currently underestimating the potential of the oral segment, and that real‑world uptake could turn out to be stronger than expected.

The newly appointed CEO, Mike Doustdar, is making Novo more effective and competitive, driving stronger commercial performance and improved internal cost efficiency. This, in turn, is supporting stronger cash generation to both reward shareholders and fund future growth initiatives.

While it’s definitely too early to draw firm conclusions, why couldn’t Mike Doustdar turn out to be a game‑changer? Lilly’s impressive performance is often attributed to David Ricks’ leadership, so in my opinion, there’s no reason why Mike Doustdar couldn’t deliver a similar impact at Novo.

Takeaway

While the approval of oral Wegovy was undoubtedly a very positive milestone, in my view, it was essential to maintain Novo’s strategic positioning. Market‑share losses to Lilly have been accelerating, and with Novo holding a leading position across ex‑U.S. markets, the looming semaglutide patent expiries in major population regions starting in 2026 create an additional significant and concerning headwind.

With a two‑ to three‑year forward P/E of around 13 and very low leverage, I still maintain my Buy rating. I believe the market has largely priced in the key challenges heading into 2026, and the current share price reflects these risks reasonably well.

While I acknowledge the headlines Novo is currently facing, we are still dealing with one of the most attractive growth markets globally, and if the new CEO executes effectively, I see no reason why Novo cannot rebuild a stronger strategic footing. That said, the BUY rating should be viewed with some caution, as I consider 2026 to be a "show‑me" year for both Novo and its new leadership.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Five Below's On-Trend Merchandise May Now Be Priced In
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Five Below (FIVE), I explained why its CEO Winnie Park was doing a great job with her merchandising strategy and why I expected the company to outperform analysts’ estimates. I think the CEO deserves credit for this discount store’s stronger-than-expected performance this quarter, because it’s not just competing on price. It’s on trend. And that’s important when you’re competing for younger shoppers who only want to spend money at the stores they love the most.

In the third quarter, Five Below reported that revenue rose 23.1% to $1.04 billion. The company reported 14.3% comps after guiding for comps in the 5%-7% range last quarter. As you might expect, it raised its guidance and now expects 6% to 8% comps in the fourth quarter and 9.4% to 10.1% comps for the full year. EPS also rose from $0.03 in 3Q 2024 to $0.66 in 3Q 2025. So, Five Below is doing great right now, and now investors can decide whether to take profits here or hold onto the stock. Obviously, expectations for Five Below are higher now. But so are its in-store prices.

Five Below’s Still Introducing Higher Price Points

On the call, Park said, “Today, value is not just about $5 and below, but ensuring we pack value into $7, $10 and $15-plus items.” This pricing strategy is obviously a risk for a store that primarily appeals to low-income parents who are trying to save money on toys. But it appears that the store’s customers understand why Five Below needs to raise its prices, and the CEO says that they’ve responded well. Five Below is trying to keep its prices on holiday merchandise affordable, but it has incurred additional expenses because of other factors, such as tariffs and labor costs.

Like I explained last time, Five Below is also using a “treasure hunt” strategy with its higher-priced merchandise. The company’s higher-priced Five Beyond products are now distributed throughout Five Below stores, so there’s no longer a separate Five Beyond department. Apparently, this strategy worked. It sounds like the new product placement strategy makes it easier for the company to focus on broader themes, such as Christmas, rather than just focusing on discounts.

Five Below also reported that it had more customers this quarter, and its customers spent more money. On the call, Park also said, “We were pleased to see this growth was led equally by increases in both comparable transactions and comparable tickets.” Right now, some stores are only reporting comps growth because they raised prices, and their traffic is down. But that’s not the case for this store.

Five Below Is Also Stockpiling Inventory

If you look at Five Below’s balance sheet, the company’s inventory rose 36% to $1.112 billion this quarter. On the call, the CEO also said that the average Five Below store is carrying 25% more inventory right now. The official explanation for this is that Five Below is stockpiling more inventory because of tariffs. But I also think that this rise in inventory could indicate additional downside or additional upside.

The bullish argument is that Five Below plans to sell its merchandise for higher prices in the future. The company will have to pay higher prices to its suppliers for products that it will sell for $10 or $15 than it would pay for products it will sell for $5. Buying these products would also increase the reported value of its inventory. So, the rise in inventory could just indicate that Five Below will maintain a pricing strategy that has already increased its comps in the first 3 quarters of 2025.

The bearish argument is that Five Below may have bought higher-priced products that its customers can’t afford. Because inventory is an asset, if the company did this, its bottom-line results would still look okay in the short term. But Five Below might have to sell its excess inventory at a discount in upcoming quarters, or even write it off entirely, and that would reduce its margins. It doesn’t look like this is happening now, but the company did write off some unsold inventory last year. So, inventory write-offs in 4Q 2025 are a risk factor for Five Below.

Five Below Has an Easy Comparison Next Quarter

Five Below’s guidance might be too low again next quarter because it has an easy comparison coming up. Winnie Park joined the company last fall, so she wouldn’t have had time to make major changes to the company’s merchandising strategy or marketing plan. So, the company was likely still following its old strategies in 4Q 2024, and its performance that quarter wasn’t great. Five Below posted -3.0% comps in 4Q 2024. But now, after reporting 14.3% comps in 3Q 2025, it’s guiding for 6%-8% comps in 4Q 2025. With an easy comparison, higher prices, and better strategies, it’s likely to surpass expectations again. Five Below’s marketing has also improved recently.

Five Below Is Now Investing in Social Media Marketing

Deep discounters, such as dollar stores, often spend very little on marketing to keep their prices low. A store that’s using the treasure-hunt strategy doesn’t really need to promote specific products, and its inventory will change frequently anyway. But now Five Below is planning to spend money on social media marketing, which makes it more like a regular toy store. Social media marketing will also make it possible for this store to collect more data on its customers than other dollar stores have.

Right now, Five Below’s mystery dumpling toys are getting attention on social media platforms such as TikTok and YouTube. The mystery dumplings are collectibles, and some of them are harder to find than others, so kids have incentives to buy more mystery boxes. And content creators are creating unboxing videos for the mystery dumplings as well. So, it appears that Five Below’s social media strategy is working well right now.

The Main Risk Is That Five Below Will Raise Its Prices Too Much

Many consumers still consider Five Below to be a dollar store like Dollar Tree (DLTR) that sells inexpensive imported products and cheap snacks. While other dollar stores are also experimenting with higher price points right now, Five Below may struggle if it begins stocking too many products at higher price points. A consumer who’s willing to spend $15 on a single item might decide to shop at an off-price store like The TJX Companies' (TJX) T. J. Maxx or HomeGoods instead.

Five Below’s Comparative Valuation Is Still Reasonable

I’ll compare Five Below to TJX and Ross Stores (ROST) again, like I did last time. After the market close on December 23, Five Below had a forward EV/EBITDA of 19.20 and a forward EV/sales of 2.54. On the same date, TJX had a forward EV/EBITDA of 22.55 and a forward EV/sales of 3.04. And Ross had a forward EV/EBITDA of 18.89 and a forward EV/sales of 2.65. So, even after the rally, Five Below is reasonably valued in comparison to peers, although the other discount stores are also trading at high multiples right now. And other dollar stores’ margins are also rising right now.

My Updated Valuation for Five Below

Five Below raised its guidance this quarter, and its 2025 guidance may still be low. So, I’ll use the top end of the company’s revised 2025 guidance this time. Its guidance calls for $4.65 billion in revenue and adjusted net income of $327 million, so its profit margin would be 7.0%. If I divide its TTM net income of $307.9 million by its TTM revenue of $4.427 billion, it has a 7.0% profit margin right now. So, I’ll divide its TTM EBITDA of $581.9 million by its TTM revenue to get a full-year EBITDA margin of 13.1%.

Last time, I projected that Five Below’s revenue would get a 10% uplift from new stores in 2026 and a 5% uplift from comps. But because of recent price increases, its comps may rise faster than I expected. So, I’ll project that it will get a 7% uplift from comps in 2026 and its full-year revenue will rise 17%. My updated estimate for 2026 sales would then be $5.120 billion. With fixed cost leverage from additional sales growth, I’ll project that its EBITDA margin will rise to 13.4% as well. So, I’d estimate that Five Below’s EBITDA will be $686 million in 2026.

At a 2026 EV/EBITDA of 18, Five Below’s EV would be $12.348 billion. After subtracting $2.01 billion in debt and adding back $525 million in cash, it would have a market cap of $10.863 billion. Five Below’s share count increased from 55.2 million to 55.3 million in 2025, so for 2026 I’ll project that its share count will rise to 55.4 million diluted shares. Dividing a market cap of $10.863 billion by 55.4 million shares gives me a one-year target price of $196 per share. That’s 4% higher than the current price of $188 per share. And the SA Quant score says Five Below is a hold right now.

Conclusion

My conclusion is that if you read my previous article and bought stock in Five Below, you should take profit now. This is still a well-managed store. Winnie Park has improved the company’s merchandising strategy by adding new products that appeal to kids. It’s clear that Asian-inspired toys like the mystery dumplings are appealing to social media users as well. And Five Below’s customers also appear to have taken the store’s price increases well. But these factors are now priced in. So, I downgrade Five Below to a hold.

data.title: Applied Optoelectronics: The Breakout Nobody Is Watching
data.description:
Investment Thesis

For so long, Applied Optoelectronics, Inc. (AAOI) was a ticker based on a transition narrative that had nothing to prove itself but was just somewhere in between traditional optics and what it claimed was hyperscalability. Since my last coverage, AAOI has skyrocketed because the company showed that its technology can address actual AI datacenter bottlenecks.

Its latest product, 400mW narrow-linewidth pump laser, is capable of delivering 800G/1.6T silicon photonics and co-packaged optics because of its high and stable optical power, overcoming power budget constraints which existing solutions cannot address and proving AAOI's advantage in this area of hyperscalers.

However, the most important development was the first volume order received for 800G hyperscalers. I believe this milestone resolves AAOI’s core uncertainty and shifts the debate from if the company belongs in AI infrastructure to how efficiently it can scale revenue, expand margins, and convert capacity into durable cash flows.

The Qualification Risk Is Gone, Execution Risk Is Not

The most significant aspect of the order from AAOI’s perspective is not the volume of the initial order of 800G but what it represents. Being qualified in the area of hyperscalers in the optical networking space is an intensive multiple year effort with a high degree of friction that is intended to eliminate the weakest suppliers in the ecosystem. Being past this hurdle qualifies AAOI not only on the basis of performance but also reliability and cost competitiveness. A significant number of optical companies qualify in the latter stages of the process. Fewer of them proceed to volume shipments.

Until December, AAOI's future was reliant on a binary outcome. A failure to build volume from qualification would have put it at risk from slower growth legacy markets. However, now that volume has begun to ship, this critical risk has been mitigated. The new risk profile is certainly more manageable, though it is no less tricky. How does AAOI plan to accomplish manufacturing and yield without sacrificing pricing and driving margins higher?

I also see a strong duration component being realized. Once integrated within hyperscale network architectures, vendors often stick around across multiple speed generations. Having a 800G presence now significantly ups the odds of being present in 1.6T deployments in the next generation, which is hard to value within short-term models.

A quick reminder, the 800G and 1.6T optics are critical because AI workloads require massive, low-latency data movement between GPUs, and slower interconnects quickly become bottlenecks. As clusters scale, bandwidth per rack must double without exploding power and cost. Applied Optoelectronics is well positioned because of its vertically integrated lasers and early hyperscaler qualification, allowing faster scaling and cost control. While competitors are close, AAOI is already shipping, which puts it slightly ahead on execution rather than technology alone.

What to Watch Next: The Proof Points That Matter After the 800G Volume Order

After the first 800G volume order, I believe the hardest part of the story is no longer belief but follow-through. Applied Optoelectronics has crossed the qualification barrier and now it must prove that this was not a one-off event but the start of a repeatable revenue engine. What gives me comfort is that management has been unusually specific about what should happen next. The roadmap is operational, not aspirational.

The initial confirmation point is Q4 revenue coming in. Q3 revenue was impacted by timing issues, not demand. Approximately $6.6 million of shipped 400G product was not recognized as revenue due to quarter-end logistics and rolled into Q4. The company also said Q4 would come in at $125–140 million in revenue, saying that the data center could grow an adequate amount of revenue organically by $25 to 40 million sequentially. In my view, hitting even the midpoint would validate that Q3 weakness was accounting noise, not a demand issue. I am far less concerned with the exact headline number than with whether data center revenue inflects decisively, as guided.

The second major component of confirmation is product mix progress. In Q3, more than 80% of data center sales were from 100G products, with limited contribution from 200G and 400G transceivers and no material contribution from 800G. That is a backward-looking indicator. Importantly, management has indicated 800G is imminent and should start seeing substantial 800G sales in Q4. That is important because 800G sales have substantially higher ASPs. A tiny number of 800G sales can have a disproportionately large effect on revenue. I will track closely for signs of 400G and 800G sales beginning to displace 100G sales in the mix.

The third confirmation point is the momentum in shipment volumes. The management team has articulated a sharp ramp, from 10-20 thousand units a month currently to 100 thousand by the end of the year and 200 thousand in mid-2026. This represents roughly a 15–16x increase in capacity over a relatively short timeframe for a company of its size. While I don't expect flawless execution, verification of the trajectory toward these goals would indicate that the growth engine is firing as intended despite revenue phasing.

The fourth confirmation point would be margin stability rather than expansion at this stage. The guidance on gross margin improvement in Q4 came in at 29-31%, which was below what the market was eagerly anticipating. In my view, the response of the stocks was wrong here. Typically, initial growth phase margins tend to contract as a consequence of learning curves and mix considerations. The key for now is margin stability as revenue momentum gathers pace.

Finally, the fact that customer breadth is the most underappreciated indicator is a positive note. The management has indicated that there are multiple additional customers that are qualifying at the 800G level at a late stage. Having a second hyperscale company indicate that they are approaching volume shipment levels would significantly mitigate concerns about concentration risk and would further cement the view that the current ramp is structural rather than a company-specific trend.

Valuation Through the Right Lens

At a stock price of $40, AAOI is no longer considered a cheap stock. The P/E ratios based on past earnings are also not meaningful because the firm is continuing to lose money. The market is valuing AAOI on forward numbers. Looking at FY2026 numbers, the stock is quoting at about 48x forward earnings. For a mature hardware company, it would be hard to explain that kind of pricing. When you are emerging from a loss phase into a growth phase, it’s not that pricey.

Revenue multiples are more informative. AAOI currently has a forward multiple of about 6x on FY2025 estimates, reducing to 3.6x on FY2026 figures if the uplift is delivered. The latter rate suggests that the market is not baking in strong out-year forecasts so much, but is rather pre-paying for their delivery and waiting for the goods.

Compared to optical stocks with more conservative growth prospects in terms of trader activity in the 3-5x sales range, AAOI appears rich but not necessarily unconcerned with fundamentals. This stock valuation can more accurately be described as a bet upon execution over the 12-18 month forward period.

Where the Risks Actually Sit

Despite the improved setup, Applied Optoelectronics remains a high-risk, high-beta investment. The most important risk is execution. Scaling production from early volumes to mass deployment can expose yield issues, supply-chain bottlenecks, or labor constraints that delay revenue and pressure margins.

Customer concentration is another concern. Near-term 800G demand is likely driven by a small number of hyperscalers. Any pause in capital spending or procurement delays could impact results quickly. Pricing pressure also remains structural. Hyperscalers exert strong negotiating power, and aggressive competition could limit AAOI’s ability to reach its targeted mid-30% gross margins.

Takeaway

I think AAOI is beyond the speculation stage and into implementation. The 800G volume order eliminates any spark of key uncertainty; now it is just sound execution. If the management can follow through on Q4 inflection points, volume growth, and mix changes, volatility is just background noise.
